result:
The Weight of Passing Clouds

I keep thinking about the way clouds refuse to linger. They roll overhead, momentarily enormous—arching like whales, bruised like peaches straining at their own seams—and then they’re rearranged by an indifferent wind and vanish. Some break apart into scavenger wisps; others just lift, thinning until the sky remembers how to be exactly the same blue it was before anything disrupted it.

I used to stretch flat on the hood of my parents’ beat-up Honda, parked at the edge of an abandoned peach orchard outside town, staring upward until the earth itself loosened its claim on me. Gravity felt negotiable then. If I stayed long enough, neck craned, breath shallow, the clouds would nothing of borders or names, tumbling east—always east—until one of them peeled off from the rest and drifted south, against the grain of its own momentum, as if it had something urgent to say to a pasture full of standing cows. I liked to imagine it might be whispering about rainfall or new calves or how the sweet spread of dusk would taste later against their rough tongues. There was a comfort in thinking that even careless vapor had errands, commitments, longings, the same as I did.

That orchard is now a subdivision with streets named after children who never played there. Morning dew has been supplanted by sprinkler systems fed by an aquifer that recedes an inch each year. I’ve googled satellite photos: peach trees replaced by peach-marketed gable roofs, bottlebrush neat as apostrophes lining driveways, community mailboxes lined up in formation like metallic hens. Still, thirty-year-old images of the same place float around if you look hard enough—earth-photographs layered over other longings—so it remains possible to toggle between then-orchard and now-subdivision the way you turn a postcard to see where the divided back was once a place to write something you hoped would arrive before homesickness did.

I think technology makes us dual citizens of time, and that can feel like a haunting. The moment I’m in is crowded with alternate nows. My phone chirps, alerting me to a memory it assembled without asking. Here’s you five years ago holding your friend’s shy baby who is now a swaggering, Lego-obsessed five-year-old; here’s a photo of coffee you forgot to drink—bloom of beige foam lacing the rim—snapped because back then you were forcing aesthetics on weekday mornings. The algorithm stitches these fragments into a little filmstrip, adding swoony piano I swear is the same tune used to advertise fiber supplements. And despite the tampering, the past arrives undeniably: vapor coalescing into something briefly solid enough to dent the mind. Then it dissipates again, obedient to whatever breeze the app has scheduled next.

We call that breeze “scrolling.”

I recently found—no, re-circulated—a paper I wrote in college on Rilke’s “thing-poems.” My younger self had been intoxicated with the idea that bottles, mirrors, a candelabra, could become seer and song. “The panther paces the pattern of his gaze,” she quoted, underlining those six words and writing YES in the margin like a shout. That freshman earnestness causes me both tenderness and second-hand embarrassment now, the way one winces at a child who, unabashed, told the grocery clerk she planned to become a cloud tamer. Yet something vital still hums within those marginalia: the conviction that objects can be solicited into personhood, that we’re only ever renting reality, that the real fee is attention. Rilke wanted to listen until things confessed their secret—like coaxing a kettle to admit the reason it insisted on whistling was loneliness.

Loneliness returns often these days like a migratory bird that remembers the exact rafter where it once nested. I am middle enough in the story of my life that friends divide sharply into two continents: those who text at 2 a.m. because insomnia makes better conversation than daylight allows, and those who have died. The island of the living keeps shrinking. Each departure rearranges the map. I picture myself redrawing borders with a dull pencil, trying to keep the names inside margins long enough for memory to dry. This is when I notice the inverse accordion of grief: it compresses or distends not based on size of loss but on sudden silences—an unused chair in the theater of group chat, the blank square where someone’s avatar once was.

And yet: there are gains. A willingness to marvel at the unbranded minutes. Yesterday I stood at the sink washing a single bowl and watched a ladybug navigate the curvature of it—a shiny planet under my kitchen sun. The bug traveled via invisible meridians, paused to investigate a residual oat flake, decided against immigration, scaled the underside of the rim. When it reached the summit, its wings unfolded as delicately as secrets long rehearsed. I held my breath, unafraid to discover we both might levitate. Then the red shell snapped shut, the wings tucked back like origami hearts, and the insect moseyed on, destination somewhere other than my drain. All this in nine seconds—commercial-free, metricless. I felt absurdly rich.

Several paragraphs ago the subject was clouds and now here we are at ladybugs. The fact that I can roam like this, in public prose, feels like a minor miracle—I mean, imagine doing it around a dentist office’s fish tank, mid-cleaning, with the hygienist’s latex fingers prowling your molars: note to self—schedule root canalling of heart… Same loose wandering occurs within relationships. The person you kiss hello has, surfing the tidal cavities of their own mind, departed for a city five thousand miles away where an eighth-grade pencil case had contained the exact shade of indigo that matches your eyes. They see you seeing them and re-zipper the detour. Lately I think connection doesn’t reside in staying relentlessly tethered, but in allowing the drift—trusting the beloved to circle back like a cloud buffeted yet unlost.

I’ve started measuring years by the last time I laughed in a way that produced immediate tears. 2017, a rooftop in Lisbon, when Manuel explained how his grandmother sampled wine using a method that involved floating a briny olive. She’d sip, spit, then swap olives like tuning forks for the palate. Laughing so hard I cried. 2020, Zoom birthday, pixelated spells of lag creating slapstick timing neither of us intended. The monitor’s frame of reference kept cropping your forehead every time you ducked. We laughed until our lungs felt turned inside-out like gloves. Laughter is a cloud, too; it suggests rain but mostly gives itself to sky.

I am preoccupied by scale. I mean how the universe overflows the postage stamp of attention we’re permitted. We make metaphors—galaxies are cells, black holes are drains—but all comparisons leak eventually. One night the power grid flickered off in my neighborhood for exactly nine minutes. No streetlamps, no TV leeching pale glow onto drapes, nojust) abrupt dark I felt simultaneously exposed and sheathed. I stepped outside. The sky revealed itself with unkind brightness—cruel abundance. It wasn’t beautiful so much as burdened with beauty, the way a constant erection might feel after a week: more than the body wished to shepherd. I understood why the ancients arranged stars into domestic fictions—shepherd, lion, spoon—because terror at cosmic grandeur must be baked into manageable pies. I almost apologized to Orion for how we’d diminished his bow to a kitchen implement.

Another night, 3:12 a.m., I discovered a half-written text unsent from last spring’s insomnia. I stared at the cursor blinking like a metronome conducted by heartbeats. The text consisted of one line: “just realized our thoughts eat us constellationally.” I must have been on fire with revelation at that hour; now the crackle’s ash. Yet I pressed send anyway—delayed archaeology arriving nine months late to a friend who has long since changed her number. Carrier reply: message failed, recipient unknown. Imagine unread blessings floating between towers, too shy to land.

I inherited my grandfather’s wristwatch. The glass is cracked in starburst fractals because he dropped it while pitching porch rail slats. I never met him—he died two years before I arrived—but in photos he’s rakish, cigarette adhering to lower lip like punctuation. The watch no longer keeps time; the mainspring snapped sometime around Reagan’s second term. Still, if you shake it gently, the second hand spasms as though refusing to concede. I’ve set moonrise on that twitch as marker of private time zone. Two nights ago, I rose to fetch water, and the phone said 2:17 a.m. but grandpa’s watch stood at 2:22. Between those five minutes I think he borrowed me—body humming at the kitchenette counter, skin thin enough to pass history like beads through unwritten silk—until I poured and drank the water. Then the second hand surrendered again. Such are the currencies of haunting: brief, counterfeit, still real tender.

Here’s a rule I’m testing: say something aloud the moment it stops holding its shape in silence. For instance—a confession: I have Googled photos of empty shoes more often than seems psychologically safe. There’s a genre of photojournalism that lingers on footwear left behind at bomb sites, marathon finish lines, border rivers. The photos insist shoes are the last body part to become self-conscious. Faces blur, interrogate, occlude; shoes simply are. Sometimes toes worn through the canvas flex like question marks. Sometimes business pumps lie neatly side-by-side, admitting nothing. I scroll, fevered, like a collector. One pair—one image—will not leave: a little red canvas sneaker, dirtied but otherwise intact, photographed in after-glow of a marketplace explosion. The shoe’s owner unknown; maybe alive. Each day that possibility contracts. So my query—what grave is Google digging inside me? Maybe the shoe is reminder that objects outlive people, by which I mean not just literally but literarily: stories entrust themselves to resonance left in territory where feet no longer are required to bear weight.

I can’t sustain grief for every catastrophe or my vertebrae would concave. So the shoe becomes idée fixe, vessel empty enough to hold more than a human heart can. It is adjacent, I theorize, to Mary Ruefle’s idea of missing, that emotion unattached to an object: we miss red even when standing in red. We miss being missed even while still physically present. Perhaps missing is a form of anticipatory conjugation for the verb “to vanish.”

A friend told me recently she experiences intimacy as dopamine weather reports: “today all the dopamine has drifted offline—check again tomorrow at 6 a.m.” She’s not being glib; she’s charting neurotransmitees with a precision that once belonged only to sailors mapping storms. We label moods with barometric confidence: serotonin storm brewing over late twenties frontal lobe, expect hesitancy but clearing by brunch. Yet I wonder if this language impoverishes experience by flattening inner tempest to analogy. Still, I borrow her phrase any time my heart’s barometer goes screwy. Dark clouds gather: I text the same friend, “System outage. Estimated repair by zeppelin. Bring snacks.”

Snacks. Sometimes I’d rather discuss snacks than souls. I have growing tenderness for pedestrian hungers. Once on a delayed train to Boston, the elderly woman across the aisle offered me marble rye and peach jam. She peeled back plastic wrapping like unveiling something holy misplaced in Tupperware. We did not exchange backstories; we shared carbohydrates. I chewed, tasting childhoods neither of us had lived—bread still warm from imaginary ovens, fruit sun-slackened on someone’s grandmother’s farm. When the conductor announced further delay, we both groaned and laughed ruefully. In that suspended car, strangers orbited by digestion, the fixability of small hungers felt profound. I wrote “communion commutes” in notebook whose cover was curling from too many coat pockets. I still recite those two words when traffic snarls on the highway home, a half-prayer to the grocery aisle saints.

Have you ever rescued a blue jay from a stairwell? I was nineteen, crashing in a sublet with floor-to-ceiling windows. The bird hurled itself against glass, beak tic-tic-tic like Morse before the telegraph knew longing. I fashioned a towel trap, corners snapped like sails; somehow caught frantic wings, carried torso upward onto the landing. When I opened the fabric sky, the jay tilted like a compass recalibrating. It took one, two beats then arrowed into morning. I stood on the fourth-floor landing feeling briefly annexed to its flight: not weightless, but weight re-distributed into the part of me that is skyward longing. Sometimes I picture that jay mid-migration, weighing less than the towel I used, bearing more than most freight trains. When I tell this story it sounds heroic. The truer version: afterward I collapsed onto carpet tiles and cried with a noise only dogs might forgive.

My therapist says depressive episodes are like weather inside furniture. I picture a dresser gradually filling with dusk. The socks become storm clouds. The shirt you loved elongates into unlit hallway. Why must mental illness always borrow home décor? I want new metaphors—sadness as un-buttered toast, as celery bell without rope, as grocery list written by someone else for items the supermarket will never stock. Instead I find myself telling friends “I am in a bad light today,” as if photography exploitation therapy existed. Maybe it does. Since everything flickers like malfunctioning fluorescents inside the skull, maybe adjusting exposure does help.

Yesterday I walked past a bakery exhaling sourdough breathing its own steam. I wanted to sit cross-legged on the curb and baptize my face in that perfume. Instead I carried on, loaves unbought. The discrepancy between desire and enactment widened with each step until it felt geological: here a seam of yearning rose sudden as fault and I faulted. Skipped lunch. Stood on street corner far from where daylight intended. I texted my mother “Tell me what to make for dinner like I’m seven.” She replied, “Cut avocado in star pattern, peanut butter on cracker boats.” I did. It tasted like a return ticket tried on for size but left on hook—proof both of fit and decision to walk away.

Clouds again: I’ve been dreaming them. Long unbroken sheets stretched taut above cities then peeling apart into tufted towns someone might fall through. In these dreams I lose my address; I live on 34th Parallel but maps show only longitudinal numbers, the latitudinal ones filed off. I knock on neighbors’ doors asking “Have you seen house-shaped like sigh?” Interpreters suggest anxiety about housing market. I think it’s simpler: I occupy too many timelines and the stitching is fraying.

This week I learned that space agencies maintain a satellite cataloguing clouds from above. Imagine bureaucratic heaven—someone filing paperwork on stray vapor. Humans are cataloguers of our own longing; we’d index heartbreak if packing slips allowed it. I imagine workers scrolling through cumulonimbus thumbnails late at night, labeling them FRAGILE. Maybe one worker pauses, zooms on a cloud shaped like her late father’s profile just above the Aegean, and unexpectedly emails it to herself labeled Dad2.jpg—contraband from memory.

My version of faith is stacking tiny moments until they resemble shelter. Last Friday night, three friends and I lit sparklers in a yard not ours—we’d gatecrashed nostalgia itself. The sparklers made inroads against dark, curling letters in illegible alphabet. Nobody checked phones. When finished, we stomped the hissing wires. Ash adhered to soles—ghostly signatures we carried back across town tire tread into quiet cars. I let ash linger; shoes pretended to be constellations for days.

Last year I discovered a documentary about a town in northern Japan where elders fashion dolls to represent residents who’ve relocated. The dolls wear sewn city uniforms, sit in classrooms no longer attended. Ghost population slowly outnumbers living. The mayor says they feel loved. This stayed with me longer than most box-office tears, because I recognized the impulse: externalize absence until absence becomes companion instead of wound. I too carry extra seats at tables inside my skull. Sometimes they nod approvingly when I mention the clouds. I think about joining this town, shipping my excess chairs, sewing little sandals for feet that will never walk again.

When I was thirteen my family traveled to Utah to watch meteor shower. We camped high enough that altitude made breath sparse coins. My father—chemist by trade, mystic by disposition—brought liquid nitrogen to flash-freeze roses then smash them like prismatic fire. While roses shattered in parking-lot neon, meteors scratched sky slow as knives across violin strings. Each streak married ooohs from crowd. My mother, usually impatient with spectacle, recorded everything on a decrepit camcorder. Years later the cassette box reads in her handwriting “Night heavens & fragile roses.” The video quality’s grain; colors bleed; meteors barely register. All that remains is soundtrack of stunned humanity taking small bites of forever. I play it sometimes when insomnia stages its coup. It’s enough.

Enough. How seldom that word lands when invited! Yet here it is: a short bench in the long hallway. I sit. In nine hours I will wake again mid-dream, error message scrolling across cortex: Memory too full to complete operation. And again I’ll delete or compress. And again the cloud-database overhead will drink condensation manufactured by a million breathers, mutate it into tomorrow’s rain forecast—70% chance—probability skewed by each exhalation I still owe the air.

This is my testament to porous borders: sky has skinned knee on earth, cloud is leaking time, we are vessels temporary as dandelion clocks with mileage. Somewhere my dead watch twitches at midnight, my living friend renumbers dopamine ZIP codes yet again, the baker kneads dawn into submission. All of it swells above and inside me like a rising loaf not yet baked. I am waiting—laughably mid-metaphor—for it to proof. In the interim, here I stand, hands dusty with cloud flour, awaiting instructions I know are already pressed against skin like handprints no glass has captured.

I too carry extra seats at tables inside my skull. Sometimes they nod approvingly when I mention the clouds. I think about joining this town, shipping my excess chairs, sewing little sandals for feet that will never walk again.

When I was thirteen my family traveled to Utah to watch meteor shower. We camped high enough that altitude made breath sparse coins. My father—chemist by trade, mystic by disposition—brought liquid nitrogen to flash-freeze roses then smash them like prismatic fire. While roses shattered in parking-lot neon, meteors scratched sky slow as knives across violin strings. Each streak married ooohs from crowd. My mother, usually impatient with spectacle, recorded everything on a decrepit camcorder. Years later the cassette box reads in her handwriting “Night heavens & fragile roses.” The video quality’s grain; colors bleed; meteors barely register. All that remains is soundtrack of stunned humanity taking small bites of forever. I play it sometimes when insomnia stages its coup. It’s enough.

Enough. How seldom that word lands when invited! Yet here it is: a short bench in the long hallway. I sit. In nine hours I will wake again mid-dream, error message scrolling across cortex: Memory too full to complete operation. And again I’ll delete or compress. And again the cloud-database overhead will drink condensation manufactured by a million breathers, mutate it into tomorrow’s rain forecast—70% chance—probability skewed by each exhalation I still owe the air.

This is my testament to porous borders: sky has skinned knee on earth, cloud is leaking time, we are vessels temporary as dandelion clocks with mileage. Somewhere my dead watch twitches at midnight, my living friend renumbers dopamine ZIP codes yet again, the baker kneads dawn into submission. All of it swells above and inside me like a rising loaf not yet baked. I am waiting—laughably mid-metaphor—for it to proof. In the interim, here I stand, hands dusty with cloud flour, awaiting instructions I know are already pressed against skin like handprints no glass has captured.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
